exercises.title: Tall and deep letters
exercises.body: Many Shavian consonants come in pairs: a tall letter for an unvoiced sound, and a deep letter for its voiced twin. Say “ffff”, then “vvvv” — feel your throat buzz on the second one? That buzz is voicing. Tall letters rise above the line; deep letters hang below it — a handy reminder, since your vocal cords sit low when they buzz.

exercises.title: Meet peep
exercises.body: The letter for peep is 𐑐. Say “p” — no buzz, so it's an unvoiced, tall letter. Its shape is just a simple stroke rising above the line.

exercises.title: A borrowed vowel: on
exercises.body: To spell real words already, borrow one vowel early: 𐑪, the short “o” heard in on and dog. You'll meet the rest of the short vowels properly in a later lesson — for now, just learn to recognize this one by sight.

exercises.caption: Sound it out — what does this say?

exercises.title: Meet bib
exercises.body: The letter for bib is 𐑚 — sort of like an upside-down peep. See them together: 𐑐 𐑚. Say “bbb”: your vocal cords buzz, so this is the deep, voiced partner of peep.

exercises.caption: Sound it out — what does this say?

exercises.prompt: Which letter is unvoiced, with no throat buzz?

exercises.prompt: 𐑚 buzzes in your throat; 𐑐 does not. What makes them a pair?
options: One is voiced, one is unvoiced
exercises.correct: One is voiced, one is unvoiced
exercises.correctLabel: One is voiced, one is unvoiced
